Thank you. Thank you.
Now, I'll just start
with
giving an example of why
it's important to be counted.
So I'm older, my parents are dead. I'm not
going to be kicked out of my home if I disclose
information about my identity. That's not the
situation for everybody. I've come here today wearing a
T-shirt saying, "Challenge biphobia."
Even wearing that in some countries in the world would be enough to get me put in
jail. But that's not Aotearoa, New Zealand.
However, it is important that we recognize why people,
particularly those who may come from different places in the world, may be
hesitant about sharing information about themselves through
administrative data,
which they are not confident about being kept
safe, secure. They don't know whether it'll be confidential.
They may be a younger person living in a conservative family
who is uncomfortable about their
parents seeing them for who they are.
This is so for the whole of the rainbow communities,
LGBTIQ+, and all the MVP, FAF,
Pasifika terms. These are issues of social
stigma. And the 2023 census,
as you will know, was the first time that people were able to
self-identify and disclose confidentially
information about themselves in a way that meant that they could be counted, and
that that information could then feed into planning for
services, for
a whole range of areas where it's important to be counted, like
173,000 people on the LGBT+ communities
in Aotearoa in 2023. And Statistics NZ
admits that's an underestimate because there are still concerns
about that confidentiality and trust.
But if we compare that to administrative data first,
and you asked agencies to give a count, you would
probably get maybe 10% of that.
As a bisexual person, I have never been asked my sexual
orientation by any government agency that has recorded my
information before that 2023 census.
So that is invisible information about me.
The same with people from
different iwi and hapū who may never have been when they're--
They might identify as Māori, but it may not go any further.
They may not
be asked that. So administrative data has got massive gaps,
and that is a risk. So as you will have seen from my...
I echo a lot of previous submissions about
even if we were living in a perfect society where people felt confident
sharing information about themselves, we're not there yet, and yet that
information is really important.
And
there's lots of things I could say, but I'll-- Oh, and just that
when I say it's really important, I used to help run hackathons using sort of data
that's been collected from sources, and I know how important it is to business, but
I'll stop there. Any questions?

cessation of the periodic census of populations and dwellings

I worked for New Zealand Statistics Department,
DataBank New Zealand, Westpac New Zealand, and then had 20 years with
Teradata Data Warehouse, where for 10 years I
governed all Asia Pacific client data projects across industries and
government.

gaps.
When clients had manual or perhaps no source systems
in place for crucial data items, data warehouse projects
were inevitably delayed or halted, and the
client's ensuing business plans and projects were also put on hold.
I'm humbled by the range and depth of expert testimony here
today.
Please consider the impact of data gaps and low-quality
data on New Zealand Government's decision-making and change
management capabilities, as so many people described.
I believe that data
and data quality are the lifeblood of every organization and every
government in our modern digital world.
And without the census data, you've heard that we stand to lose the
kete, or basket, or spine perhaps that connects,
and contains all our people information, identifying individuals,
dwelling location, ages, relationships to their households,
and in most cases, that's their family.We'll lose
granular data about individuals and families
coming directly from people. That's you and me, so
it's more accurate and probably the most highly trusted data in government
and beyond. It's more than a century
of continuous high-quality
longitudinal data, and data that
directly affects
electoral boundaries, foundation of our democracy.
Apart from the census, no government department counts and locates all people
present in New Zealand, as you've heard.
And I don't think I can be represented in the same way as
a taxpayer, a beneficiary, a patient, a land
holder. Government departments hold only slim
slices of data, as we would say.
New Zealand's population statistics are key pieces of our national
infrastructure. I view the census data as playing a pivotal
role

the population strategy that they were talking about.
I can say more, but to conclude, along with the
repercussions that experts here describe, it's the lack of
process and rigor that I've heard this morning in reaching this
point that is perhaps most disturbing.
Please reconsider our loss of population and family data,
and commission an independent expert review of the decisions and
options regarding the census before it is
abandoned. Thank you very much for listening.

world depression,

I'm the executive director of We Create.
We Create is the alliance of New Zealand's creative industry
associations and creative organizations, representing
over 30,000 Kiwi creatives and creative businesses
across all creative industries, from games, to music, to screen, to publishing,
fashion, architecture, advertising, design, performing, and visual arts.
We note that the creative sector is highly digital in how the products and
services we make and sell are created, marketed,
distributed, and consumed. We're here today because this bill
matters, not just to the government and statisticians, but to every
creative professional in this country whose work and value is
currently mostly invisible to government data systems.
We Create has engaged with the New Zealand government data system for over a
decade. We commissioned NZIR to produce the first
comprehensive valuation of the creative sector in 2016
because government data could not tell that story.
In 2026, it still cannot tell it accurately.
As it currently operates, the data system does not see us, and without
deliberate intervention, this bill will lock in and magnify that
invisibility.
The creative industries are not a niche concern for the data system.
The Ministry for Culture and Heritage reports that the sector contributed
18.5 billion to GDP in 2025 and
employed 122,000 New Zealanders or
4.4% of the total workforce.Creative sectors are
defined by UNCTAD and many advanced economies as
high-value economic contributors.
In New Zealand, the Cabinet-approved Amplify Creative and Cultural Strategy
sets out an evidence framework as a core government commitment and
deliverable under the strategy. That commitment cannot be delivered if the
underlying data infrastructure is broken.
The data system used by Infometrics to produce data for MCH
continues to draw from data systems that do not accurately reflect the
creative sector and occupations as they exist today,
and have no mechanism for keeping up with technology change.
If we transition to an admin data-first census model without fixing
the foundations of the existing system, we will entrench what is
already broken.
The bill's regulatory impact statement acknowledges that certain communities will
be harder to measure under the new model and may require tailored
solutions. The creative sector meets this definition in every
respect. Let me be specific about why.
The freelance and portfolio work reality.
The creative sector has double the number of freelancers and contractors of any
other sector in the economy. Our workforce is characterized by
multi-occupation, project-based, and good structured employment.
Admin data systems built around the employer-employee
model are structurally ill-equipped to capture this reality.
Most of government policy, including in education and vocational
training, is still predicated on an employment model that is simply
outdated for our sector. That must change, and this bill is
the place to begin fixing it.
The IRD single occupation model
currently allows only a single occupation to be reported.
For a sound engineer who also teaches, composes, and earns royalties from a
streaming platform, that single field is not just
incomplete, it is misleading. If IRD data becomes a
pillar of the new census model, these gaps become structural features,
not temporary limitations.
The classification code problem is still unresolved.
ANZSIC and ANZSOC codes must be updated to reflect the realities of
modern and evolving creative and digital economy.
The census transition should not proceed without addressing these
gaps. The digital industries and the rear view mirror
problem. Every industry in our membership is digital, yet existing
data systems do not capture the breadth or depth of value created by
digital creative work. The new census model must build in
live mechanisms to capture emerging digital industries and
occupations on an ongoing basis, not simply take a
snapshot of where the economy was at the last classification review.
The investment and infrastructure gap.
We want to be direct with the committee about something that underlies all of this.
The minister's announcement framed this bill primarily around cost reduction.
Where Create's concern is that building a new census model on admin data
that already misrepresents and underrepresents the creative sector
and doing so to save money is a false economy.
We are skeptical that data of sufficient quality and accuracy sits in
government agencies, and that it can be extracted into the data system
using resources within agencies' existing budget lines.
Data is an economic asset. Its value is seen most
clearly in big tech and artificial intelligence companies, where
vast volumes of data underpin revenue at scale.
The New Zealand government has not sufficiently prioritized or invested in data
systems, particularly in the last three decades, as technology
change made better data capture more feasible, not less.
We have seen
what a deliberate and resourced approach to data can
deliver. When Toi Māi, the Workforce Development Council for Creative,
Cultural, Tech and Recreation sectors was established, it invested in a
dedicated data and insights team.
That team embedded data thinking at the very start of every project.
In many cases, data and insights were captured that would otherwise have not
existed and were subsequently useful well beyond their original
purpose. That is the level of intentionality and investment
that is needed across government if the outcomes that this bill
sets out are to be achieved.
Here is what we are asking the committee to do.
That Stats NZ formally designate the creative sector as a harder
to measure community, requiring tailored data collection solutions,
and that this designation be written into the transition planning, not
left to discretion.
Secondly, that the outstanding classification issues with ANZSIC and ANZSOC codes
be resolved before the 2030 census transition.
Thirdly, that We Create's Data and Insights working group be formally recognized
as a co-design partner in the design of the Census Attribute
Survey.
Fourthly, that the single occupation limitation on IRD admin data be
addressed as part of the cross-government admin data improvement program.
And finally, that Stats NZ be required to track and publicly report on
creative sector data quality as part of the periodic review
obligations under the amended act.
In closing, the creative sector is one of Aotearoa's most dynamic economic
contributors and one of its least visible in official data.
We've been making this point to government for over a decade.
This bill is an opportunity to finally close that gap or to lock it in for
another generation. We are asking you to choose the former.
We're grateful for the opportunity to present to you today, and I'd welcome any
questions you might have.
Thank you, Paula, and particularly recognizing your sector.
It is huge and growing, and hopefully grows even more rapidly.
So thank you for a bit of a passionate plea about that. Any questions?
Got one from Camilla Village.
Thank you. We've heard from some other submitters who have concerns about the data,

So my question for you really is, if these changes aren't made,

It's stagnation in government policy development.
I can give you an example. We're still not counting digital exports
in this country, so the value of what the creative
sector is exporting isn't visible in government data.
When you're looking at what shapes our industry, we're still,
another example, if you're a game developer, you're a software

the workforces are,
or valuable things like
what's happening with exports. That leads to policy developments that
we're constantly having to go, "What about us?" in.
And being present in places where really the data should be telling the
story about where government policy and support should be
heading. As industry, we don't feel that we should have to
invest quite so much time in doing that to help government do its
job.
All right, good. I've got a question from Carl Bates.

data collection for industries like yours could achieve that outcome?

I think there is way more we need to do with

I'm not suggesting that that solely sits with government, but government obviously
has a key role in holding core data about
what's happening in the country and the economy as it relates to policy
development.
We have our own industry
who correlate their own data. You'll know from what
the committee's been looking at that getting data doesn't come
cheaply. So we've got industries with
small budgets trying to create their own data in the absence of government
data. What we need is that government has the core data, and that
anything the industry is doing is over and above that in an area that's of
particular interest to the industry.
The government having a solid base of core data, I think is the
important thing.

All right. I'll introduce myself first. I'm Claire Johnston.
I'm a regional councilor, first term,
representing the Porirua, Tawa area, but this presentation is, of course,
on the whole region. Why are we presenting on this bill?
We're doing this because as an organization making evidence-based
investment decisions for our communities means that we have to
actually understand our data to make those decisions. It's pivotable.
Robust data matters.
We're responsible as a regional council for long-term network and
place-based planning across transport, land use,
infrastructure, emergency and hazard management, and of
course, for resilience.
We plan and invest over a 20 to 50 year timeframe,
and often for assets that will span across many generations.
Our decisions balance the growth, safety, equity,
and environmental protection, and should always be value for
money.
We're required by legislation to base our decisions on sound evidence and
robust data. As a councilor, I always expect
evidence and robust data.
Regional spatial planning and how we plan our region and how we consolidate
urban growth around existing growth corridors, how we pair
urban growth with economic development, how we stage growth over time,
all matter.
The bulk water supply is another issue.
How do we anticipate population growth with water supply
when we make these investment decisions?
Water, of course, is up for debate at the moment, but it's really important you
have consistent information and understand where your region's going.
Rural and urban flood protection, the level we
build at flood protection and schemes, is that one to 50
protection? Is it one to 500 protection?
Or do we
take into account, since January, I think we've had
72 incidents of national or district
emergency management issues.
Public transport is big for the Greater Wellington Regional Council.
It's probably the area that I get asked the most questions
about-We have to have really good
information, a really good understanding of our population and where our population
works, and where our population goes to school, and where they get
to.
So why does it matter to us? Why census?
GWRC combines the latest science with historical data,
current information, future forecasting.
We also have to understand the patterns.
We use these assessments to develop our policy to make the
investment decisions, land use planning, and as I said,
emergency planning.
We can't function on fragmented
population data.
Disaggregated and administrative data sets only work when
anchored to a strong, centrally collected national
population count.
I've personally watched, over the years,
data that isn't collected properly or it's inconsistent in its
collection. I've seen it particularly in economic data.
If you weaken the national collection,
it leaves regional councils carrying planning, emergency, fiscal,
and equity consequences without a reliable
evidence base.
Census data presents a single source of truth that we
and our communities and iwi can build and get behind
when we're making decisions.
Central population statistics are critical to regional
infrastructure.
They provide a single authoritative population baseline that we rely
on.
Decisions to invest in 30 to 100-year lifespans must be based on
good data.
Emergency management depends on that data.
Hazard management depends on it. So does statutory planning,
and legal defensibility is weakened if you don't have
evidence. The costs and the
responsibility could fall onto councils if we have no
solution. That concerns me because collecting data is
imperative. You can't have inconsistency.
You can't have one region collecting data one way and
one collecting it another. There must be consistency.
It also affects you as policymakers.
If you can't see the whole,
you risk fragmentation in your
own policy decisions.
So the bottom line for us is weakening national population
collection does not empower regions.
It fragments the system, it increases the risk, and it
undermines some of those critical infrastructural decisions.
We do support innovation, and the use of administrative data
needs to improve.
We acknowledge there have been problems with efficiency and
timeliness.
However, it's essential that the new model continues to provide accurate,
trusted, and sufficiently granular population data at
a regional, territorial, and community level, as well
as your national level.
To conclude, it comes down to the quality and detail of the data.
We're reminded that New Zealand does not have a great track record
in large-scale data projects.
The challenge to this select committee is to ensure that for the transition,
organizations like Greater Wellington will continue to have robust and
reliable data from which to make our decisions.
Flying blind in the future does not really bear
thinking about. We cannot do that.
